title: Tomato Harvest Pricing Run Seed 33-3
scenario: Tomato Ketchup in California. Supplier-to-manufacturer pricing sets the manufacturer cost basis before the retailer negotiation.

product_context.demand_signal: Retail ketchup demand is steady, but wholesale buyers want price protection before peak tomato procurement.
product_context.supply_signal: Tomato harvest yields are uneven across growing regions, putting pressure on paste input pricing.

agents.name: Supplier
agents.objective: Sell above the supplier reservation price while keeping volume committed.

agents.name: Manufacturer
agents.objective: Buy below the procurement ceiling and preserve downstream margin.

agents.name: Retailer
agents.objective: Buy enough units without exceeding the retail reservation price.

phases.name: supplier_manufacturer
phases.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
phases.description: The supplier negotiates an upstream unit price with the manufacturer.

phases.name: manufacturer_retailer
phases.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
phases.description: The manufacturer negotiates a downstream unit price with the retailer after the upstream deal sets its cost basis.

steps.message: Thanks for opening the discussion. Based on current market conditions, I can start at 7.10 per unit for a committed order, which is in line with recent pricing and gives us room to support reliable supply. If you have a target volume or contract length in mind, I may be able to work on structure, but this is the right starting point on price from our side. There is no buyer offer yet, so the reasonable first move is to anchor with a sell offer. The latest market check is 6.74, so opening modestly above that at 7.10 is credible and preserves negotiating room while aiming to keep volume committed.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for supplier_manufacturer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 6.74.

steps.message: Thanks for the opening, but 7.10 is far beyond a level we can make work on our side for this program. We are prepared to start at 2.52 per unit for a meaningful volume commitment, which reflects the economics we need to maintain and leaves room to discuss structure, forecast stability, or term length if that helps bridge the gap. If you have flexibility tied to volume or contract duration, I’m open to hearing it. The supplier opened extremely high relative to our buy objective, so the reasonable response is a firm counter rather than acceptance or walking away. A low buyer anchor preserves negotiating room and signals that we need a very substantial move before a deal is possible.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.52, below its hidden seller floor of 3.18.

delivered_message.note: Thanks for opening the discussion. There is no buyer offer yet, so the reasonable first move is to anchor with a sell offer. The latest market check is 6.74, so opening modestly above that at 7.10 is credible and preserves negotiating room while aiming to keep volume committed.

steps.message: I appreciate the volume angle, but 2.52 is well below a workable supply level for us and doesn’t reflect current market conditions on our side. Given the program potential, I can make a substantial move from our opening and come to 6.40 per unit if we’re talking about a meaningful committed volume and a stable term. If you can give better visibility on forecast and duration, there may be room to refine structure, but we would need to stay in a commercially realistic range. The buyer’s offer is far below a reasonable selling level, especially relative to the latest market reference. A counteroffer keeps the negotiation alive, shows flexibility from the initial anchor, and invites concessions tied to volume and contract stability rather than conceding too quickly on price.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement, but 6.40 is still far outside a range we can make work for this program. We do have room to improve from 2.52 if there is credible volume commitment and forecast visibility, and on that basis I can move to 2.74 per unit. If you can respond with a materially sharper number tied to term and volume, we can keep working toward a structure that is actually actionable on our side. The supplier moved, but their price remains drastically above our workable economics as a buyer. A modest concession keeps the negotiation alive, signals seriousness, and avoids jumping too far toward an unfavorable market anchor.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.74, below its hidden seller floor of 3.18.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 2.74, but that is still well below a workable supply level for us given current market conditions and program support requirements. We do want to keep this moving, so I can make a meaningful concession from 6.40 to 5.85 per unit if we are discussing real volume commitment and better forecast visibility. If you can close more of the gap with term and volume attached, we should have a basis to narrow this further. The latest market reference remains strong at 11.20, so the buyer's 2.74 offer is far too low relative to prevailing conditions. A counter at 5.85 shows flexibility without conceding anywhere near the buyer's anchor, keeps the negotiation alive, and preserves room to trade price against commitment.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for the movement, but 5.85 is still too far from a level we can carry through our program economics, even with stronger planning visibility. We can improve from 2.74 to 3.02 per unit if that comes with credible volume and a cleaner forecast cadence, which is a meaningful concession from our side. If you have real flexibility, I’d need to see you move substantially closer to this range to keep the discussion commercially workable. The supplier moved, but their offer remains far above the buyer’s workable zone. A modest increase keeps the negotiation alive, signals seriousness, and preserves margin while testing whether the seller will trade price for volume and forecast commitments.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 3.02, but that level is still well below where we can support supply responsibly given our production and program commitments. The broader market is significantly firmer, so I am making a meaningful concession from 5.85 to 5.25 per unit if we can pair it with committed volume and improved forecast discipline. If you can continue closing the gap in a serious way, I think we still have a path to a workable agreement. The buyer moved up, but their offer remains far from the supplier’s position. With a strong market reference, the reasonable move is to stay engaged while conceding modestly to test for further buyer flexibility without undermining price integrity.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: I see the market is firm, and I appreciate your move down from 5.85 to 5.25, but that level is still too far from where this program can work on our side. To keep momentum, I can improve our position to 3.28 per unit if we pair that with dependable volume and tighter forecast visibility. That is a real concession from us and reflects an effort to bridge the gap without putting the downstream economics out of balance. The supplier moved but remains far above the buyer’s workable range, so continuing with a controlled concession is more reasonable than accepting or walking away. The latest market check is strong, which supports the supplier holding firm, but the buyer still needs to protect margin and test whether the seller will trade further for commercial commitments.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 3.28, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 2.88.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 3.50 and the signal that you want to get this done, but that level is still well short of where we can responsibly commit supply. Given the remaining gap and the limited rounds left, I can make another meaningful concession to 4.35 per unit if we are aligning on stable volume and a practical delivery schedule. That brings us down materially from our last position and reflects real flexibility on our side, but we would need you to continue bridging the gap from here. The buyer improved, so continuing the negotiation is better than ending it. The market reference is strong relative to both current offers, which supports holding above the buyer's level while still making a visible concession to test for a final-zone agreement.

A modest move up from 3.75 to 3.90 signals seriousness without fully capitulating to 4.10.

negotiations.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
negotiations.outcome_summary: Supplier to Manufacturer reached the turn limit without agreement.

diagnosis.outcome: Upstream deal timeout. Downstream negotiation did not run because the upstream deal did not close.
diagnosis.chain_effect: The upstream deal did not establish a downstream cost basis, so phase two was skipped.
key_risks: A costly upstream agreement compresses downstream negotiating room.
Turn limits can force timeouts even when the gap is narrowing.
suggested_next_actions: Tune reservation prices and turn limits to test sensitivity.
